results.title: CEX vs DEX: Which Crypto Exchange Is Better in 2026? Pros, Cons, and Hidden Risks
results.author: Yuri Molchan
results.content:
Talking about a CEX exchange versus a DEX exchange once felt like religion in crypto. Speed mattered here – alongside help when things broke, money from banks flowing in, tidy graphs on screens. Over there, control of your keys took priority, along with free trading access and the elimination of layers between buyers and sellers. Now, in 2026, there is less belief and more weighing of what dangers you face while staying honest about them.
Most people trust centralized exchanges since they resemble banks. Yet some find peace in keeping their keys on a decentralized platform. Comfort does not mean safety here. Hacks might break a CEX, just like empty reserves or blocked access. Poor programming could wreck a DEX, along with counterfeit coins or mistaken permissions. Mistakes happen either way.
Contents
- 1.What Is a Crypto Exchange?
- 2.What Is a Centralized Exchange (CEX)?
- 3.What Is a Decentralized Exchange (DEX)?
- 4.CEX vs DEX: Key Differences at a Glance
- 5.Security Risks of Centralized Exchanges
- 6.Security Risks of Decentralized Exchanges
- 7.CEX vs DEX Fees: Which Is More Cost-Effective?
- 8.Privacy and Regulatory Compliance
- 9.CEX vs DEX for Different Types of Crypto Users
- 10.Common Errors When Choosing a Crypto Exchange
- 11.FAQ
What Is a Crypto Exchange?
A place online lets users trade digital coins using real-world currency. One kind links banking systems to cryptocurrency networks. Another type runs entirely inside blockchain technology, shifting value between personal accounts via coded agreements.
How Crypto Exchanges Facilitate Digital Asset Trading
Inside a firm’s own platform, a central cryptocurrency exchange links people who want to buy with those looking to sell. Trades on a distributed exchange move via smart contracts on the blockchain, often shifting funds straight between personal wallets.
The Role of Exchanges in the Crypto Ecosystem
Where people look, value flows. These platforms shape what moves fast in markets. The speed of access depends on their design. Barriers between traditional cash and crypto live here.
Related: Is Crypto Dead? Fear & Greed Index Hits Critical Lows as Investor Panic Spreads
What Is a Centralized Exchange (CEX)?
Running things behind the scenes, a centralized exchange – often called a CEX – operates under one company’s authority. Think of it like this: people sign up, put money into their account, then buy or sell digital coins using that site. Oversight lands with the firm itself, which holds responsibility for keeping user funds safe.
How Centralized Exchanges Work
Most times, trades move through internal ledgers – speed shows right away on screen. Once money arrives, digital records update without delay.
Key Features of Centralized Platforms
Most people find it easier to add cash through banks on centralized exchanges. These spots often come with phone-friendly programs plus help when things go sideways. Buying right away shows up clearly here, along with betting on price swings or locking coins for rewards. Some even handle number-crunching tasks like tax reports without extra steps. When new users weigh CEX against DEX, comfort wins every time.
Leading Examples of CEXs
Most well-known centralized exchanges are Coinbase, Binance, Kraken, OKX, Bybit, and Bitstamp – though availability shifts by location and what services they offer. Since rules differ, people should review permits, how withdrawals work, available funds, costs, and which company actually handles their account.
What Is a Decentralized Exchange (DEX)?
Trading happens directly between people using code that runs itself, no middleman needed. Your coins stay in your pocket the whole time – no giving them to some firm. Software handles deals once you approve with a digital signature. Ownership stays with you because the record updates right on the blockchain. No gatekeeper watches over funds; rules are baked into the system. You hold keys, make moves, and results lock in publicly.
How Decentralized Exchanges Work
Once linked, a wallet lets the user pick two tokens. After checking the rate, they confirm the transfer when required. Signing off on the trade comes next. Blockchain systems carry out the exchange via decentralized networks. Execution happens once signals pass through nodes.
The Role of Smart Contracts in DEX Trading
Liquidity lives inside smart contracts – these bits of code figure out pricing, send trades where they need to go, and make sure everyone follows the rules. Runs on its own, no old-style middleman needed, yet stumbles hard if a signature fails or a token shows up broken.
Leading Examples of DEXs
Uniswap, Curve, PancakeSwap, Balancer, Sushi, Jupiter, and dYdX stand out among DEXs. Not everyone handles trades the same way – certain ones prioritize basic token exchanges, whereas some build around stablecoin pools, path optimization, or advanced tools for users trading without central oversight.
CEX vs DEX: Key Differences at a Glance
Usage of a centralized vs a decentralized exchange has always been a big choice and controversy. What separates CEX from DEX isn’t just structure – it’s where you place your limits. With a centralized exchange, ease comes along with reliance on others. On the flip side, decentralized platforms hand over authority, yet expect careful habits. Trust shapes one path; personal rigor defines the other.
Read more: How to Get Started Buy Crypto with DEX Wallet: A Beginner’s Guide
Money stays with the platform. Yet you keep keys in your own hands. Logging needs account setup, often with ID checks. While connecting means linking your digital container directly. Markets bustle near popular coins. Though flow depends on how much sits pooled. Costs appear when moving or exchanging. Plus network charges tag along swaps. The main risk depends on who holds the assets. But mistakes can start from clicks or hidden flaws.
| Category | CEX | DEX | 
|---|
| Custody | Platform holds funds | User controls wallet | 
| Access | Account and usually KYC | Wallet connection | 
| Liquidity | Strong on major pairs | Strong where pools are deep | 
| Fees | Trading and withdrawal fees | Swap fees and gas | 
| Main Risk | Counterparty failure | User and code error | 
Custody of Assets
Most of the time, the person who holds the keys decides what kind of exchange it is. A central hub keeps those secrets locked away internally. With a peer-to-peer setup, access stays in your hands at all times.
Account Registration and KYC Requirements
Registration pops up at nearly every centralized exchange, along with ID verification. While decentralized platforms skip the usual sign-up, activity isn’t invisible – blockchain records linger, plus certain interfaces block access by location.
Trading Experience and Ease of Use
Most people find CEX platforms easier because they offer clearer layouts, better help systems, multiple ways to place trades, plus tools that fix mistakes fast. Jumping into a DEX means learning how wallets work, picking the correct networks, allowing token access, managing fees, handling price shifts during swaps, using cross-chain links, and then double-checking every coin before acting.
Liquidity and Market Depth
Most of the time, big coins like Bitcoin move more easily through central exchanges. Ethereum usually follows that pattern too. Stablecoins? They tend to stick close to those same hubs. Newer tokens built for DeFi, though – they show up on decentralized markets before anywhere else. Where a coin lives affects where it flows best.
Available Trading Pairs
Some exchanges only show certain tokens. When trading happens, a decentralized platform might include nearly any asset – opening doors, yet inviting trouble. Liquidity shows up, options grow, danger grows too.
Related: Crypto Exchange Battle 2026: Binance vs Bybit — Where Do Traders Prefer to Trade?
Transaction Speed and Settlement
Most times, CEX deals seem immediate since numbers shift behind the scenes. When using a DEX, each trade waits for blockchain confirmation – how fast that happens relies on traffic at that moment.
| Factor | CEX | DEX | 
|---|---|---|
| Custody | Exchange holds user funds | User controls funds through a wallet | 
| Access | Account registration and often KYC | Wallet connection, usually no classic account | 
| Ease of Use | Easier for beginners | Better for experienced DeFi users | 
| Liquidity | Usually deeper on major assets | Depends on pools and network activity | 
| Main Risk | Hacks, insolvency, frozen withdrawals | Smart contract bugs, fake tokens, wallet mistakes | 
Security Risks of Centralized Exchanges
Security-wise, comparing CEXs to DEXs isn’t straightforward. While one exchange might employ experts in protection, offline fund storage, live oversight, plus regulatory adherence, its pooled user assets create temptation. That central hub? Harder to breach, maybe, yet collapse from one hit.
Exchange Hacks and Custodial Risks
When hackers break into a central exchange, people might get locked out – despite having strong passwords. Solid services lower danger using offline wallets along with tight checks. Yet holding assets together means everyone shares the risk, no matter what.
Insolvency and Counterparty Exposure
Trading platforms might collapse even when no one breaks in. Poor loans here, secret debt there – these eat away at stability. Hidden risks pile up behind the scenes. Affiliates running wild? That turns exchanges into ticking issues. Are reserves too thin? Then trust slips fast.
Regulatory and Account-Freezing Risks
When rules tighten, some platforms block access. Withdrawals might stall without warning. Tokens disappear from trading screens overnight. Rules meant to help can also lock money in place. Protection shifts into restriction – quietly, suddenly.
Security Risks of Decentralized Exchanges
Most danger on DEXs hits close, tangled in code. Even without holding your coins, the system puts full weight on you. Once you approve a deal, nobody waits to undo it. Help desks vanish when signatures lock in.
Smart Contract Vulnerabilities
A system spread across many computers runs using coded agreements, outside data feeds, connection tools, and sometimes guided by human oversight. Checks by experts can spot issues – yet flaws might remain hidden afterward.
Rug Pulls and Fake Tokens
Openness in DEXs allows anyone to create and trade tokens. Because of this freedom, impostor tokens pop up easily – names get duplicated, minting stays concealed, sales are locked, liquidity vanishes overnight. Fake projects slip through without warning, riding on access meant for everyone.
Wallet Security and User Responsibility
With a DEX, your wallet acts as your account. Start by losing the seed phrase, then add signing a harmful contract, maybe toss in linking to a counterfeit page – your crypto is gone just like that. Mistakes stick around when recovery isn’t built in.
Read more: Top 5 DEX Wallets in 2026: Which Crypto Wallet Is Best for DeFi and Swaps?
CEX vs DEX Fees: Which Is More Cost-Effective?
Hidden charges hide behind every corner when comparing CEX and DEX trades. Spreads stretch wider than they seem at first glance. Slippage creeps in during volatile moments. Gas costs rise without warning. Withdrawal fees appear after the main event. Bridge transfers add another layer that most forget. Being trapped in a position carries its own silent toll.
Trading Fees
Pricing at centralized exchanges swings based on whether you’re placing orders or filling them – big traders sometimes pay less. On decentralized platforms, costs come from liquidity pools when swapping tokens. One option beats the other only when the amount traded lines up just right with available market supply.
Deposit and Withdrawal Costs
Most of the time, a centralized exchange adds fees when you put money in by card or take it out. Sometimes specific ways of paying come with extra charges, too. With a decentralized platform, there is no standard way to add cash upfront. However, shifting assets from one wallet to another – especially across different networks – often incurs fees.
Network Fees and Gas Costs
Most people diving into decentralized exchanges overlook gas fees. When traffic spikes, even tiny trades might not be worth it. Cheaper blockchains offer relief – yet bring unfamiliar dangers along. Networks with lower costs aren’t always safer in practice.
Privacy and Regulatory Compliance
Now privacy matters most on crypto exchanges. Because oversight fits better with centralized systems. Even though decentralized ones seem freer at first glance. Yet transparency comes baked into blockchain-ledger always.
Privacy on Centralized Exchanges
Most of the time, a centralized exchange gathers personal info like ID papers, financial records, hardware identifiers, along with logs of past trades. Legal teams might pull that data when needed, yet oversight comes at the cost of anonymity. While safeguards against scams get stronger, private ownership fades behind layers of required disclosure.
Privacy on Decentralized Exchanges
Even without showing ID, most DEX platforms leave traces. Every move shows up on-chain. Firms start connecting dots when money moves in or out. Patterns grow clear across transfers between chains. Ownership of digital items adds another clue. Doing similar things over time makes links easier to spot.
AML and KYC Considerations
Expect stricter checks on cash gateways, dollar-pegged tokens, complex trades, big outflows soon. Behind-the-scenes code might stay live, though user screens could get pickier. Pressure builds where money meets blockchains. Interfaces adapt before engines do.
CEX vs DEX for Different Types of Crypto Users
Which works better – centralized exchanges or decentralized ones? That choice shifts per person. New people might lean one way. Traders often pick another path. Folks deep in DeFi tend toward distinct setups. Holders planning years adjust differently. Some of the smartest mix pieces from each world.
Beginners
Most new users find it simpler to begin using a CEX since getting in, navigating around, and asking for help tend to be smoother. Yet trouble often shows up when they keep large amounts sitting there post-purchase.
Active Traders
Most folks who trade a lot lean toward centralized exchanges because getting in and out fast feels smoother. Yet when servers crash, accounts get wiped by sudden price swings, or cashing out hits a wall – those perks fade quickly.
DeFi Users
Most people using DeFi rely on decentralized exchanges – those platforms host loans, rewards, voting tokens, and even new coin launches. Yet even skilled traders might end up losing funds if they trade a counterfeit asset.
Long-Term Investors
Buying things takes different thought than keeping them safe over time. For trades and getting money in, a central platform works fine. When it comes to big amounts, though, better protection makes sense. How you hold matters just as much as what you buy.
Common Errors When Choosing a Crypto Exchange
Skipping steps feels faster at first. A smooth screen, tiny costs, or some shiny coin pulls attention away. Yet those dull safety habits? They’re what keep wallets full. Most slip-ups begin by ignoring them.
Prioritizing Low Fees Over Security
A shaky foundation won’t improve just because costs are low. Look into past breaches before studying pricing charts – where it operates matters, so does proof of funds, how fast money leaves, and what users say.
Ignoring Liquidity and Slippage
Most times, value looks solid until you test it. Pool depth matters more than the number shown on screen. Look at how much was moved recently instead of trusting the display. Order books reveal what numbers actually wait behind. Slippage tweaks expose whether trades hold up when pushed. Real movement hides in the details that few bother to check.
Storing Large Balances on Exchanges
Trading happens on exchanges. Switching currencies works there, too. Moving money in or out uses traditional banking paths. Liquidity stays tied to those platforms. Storing crypto long-term? That belongs somewhere else. When others hold the keys, you do not really own it.
FAQ
Is a DEX Safer Than a CEX?
Most trust moves from exchange to personal control when using a DEX. Yet safety isn’t guaranteed just by that shift. Instead, exposure spreads into how wallets are managed, into code behind contracts, into what tokens are chosen, and also into each choice a person makes on their own.
Can Beginners Use a Decentralized Exchange?
True – beginning with the basics makes sense. Start by getting comfortable with how wallet backups work. Picking the right network comes next, something often overlooked. Checking tokens carefully can prevent costly mistakes. Slippage settings matter more than many think at first. Gas fees pop up everywhere, so understanding them helps. Handling approvals wisely keeps funds safer.
Do DEXs Require KYC?
Not every DEX needs ID checks as banks do. Still, certain websites might limit countries, filter wallet types, or adjust who can join when laws shift. What seems open today could narrow tomorrow. Rules evolve, so access sometimes follows. Even without paperwork, entry isn’t always guaranteed. Oversight grows, and gateways respond. Flexibility exists – until it doesn’t.
Which Fees Are Lower: on CEX or DEX?
Most times, big trades save money on a centralized exchange. Sometimes swapping tokens works out better using a decentralized one – especially when network fees stay low. Spreads play a role. So does how much the price moves during execution. Withdrawal charges matter just as much. Trade volume changes the outcome, too.
Can You Trade Every Cryptocurrency on a DEX?
True, though not always. Decentralized exchanges tend to feature a wider range of freshly launched or lesser-known tokens compared to centralized ones. This expanded selection opens doors to counterfeit assets, shallow trading volume, and greater chances of fraud. While variety grows, so do the dangers hiding within.
Truth be told, picking between CEX and DEX isn’t about loyalty to a team. Centralized platforms shine with easier cash deposits, faster trades, solid customer help, plus stronger order books. Running your own wallet, tapping straight into blockchain tools, jumping into DeFi actions – those tasks favor decentralized setups. In 2026, sharp users pick the tool that fits the job, not just brand names.
results.summary:
Choosing between centralized and decentralized cryptocurrency exchanges in 2026 requires balancing the convenience and support of managed platforms against the personal control and security risks of blockchain-based trading.

### Key Points
- Centralized exchanges (CEXs) like Coinbase and Binance offer user-friendly interfaces, customer support, and easier fiat-to-crypto conversions.
- Decentralized exchanges (DEXs) such as Uniswap and dYdX allow users to retain custody of their private keys and trade directly via smart contracts.
- CEXs face risks including platform insolvency, regulatory account freezes, and potential security breaches of pooled assets.
- DEXs carry risks related to smart contract vulnerabilities, counterfeit tokens, and the permanent loss of funds due to user error.
- Transaction costs vary significantly based on trading volume, network gas fees, and market liquidity depth.

### Why it Matters
Understanding the trade-offs between these platforms is essential for managing digital assets safely and efficiently in an evolving regulatory landscape. Investors must weigh the ease of use provided by centralized firms against the self-sovereignty and technical risks inherent in decentralized finance.

results.title: Half of workers worry AI will still take their job — as new report claims use of agents soars 90% in just a year
results.author: Craig Hale
results.content:
- Nearly half of UK workers fear job losses, but agent usage continues to grow
- Workers are also worried about workplace monitoring
- Human oversight remains key, but worker retraining is a must
New data from GMB Union has revealed that nearly half (48%) of UK workers worry AI will take their job amid broader AI rollouts, which could be reducing uptake.
Nearly a third (29%) said their employers have introduced AI tools, with a quarter (26%) noting that artificial intelligence is now performing tasks they would normally do themselves, fueling the fears.
But beyond administrative support and potential job automation, a clear misunderstanding of the tech could be driving other workplace fears.
Workers concerned about job cuts despite increasing AI adoption
For example, nearly half (48%) believe AI is being used to monitor or track them, introducing new surveillance and performance monitoring fears.
GMB likens its findings to previous shifts like the advent of the internet – union reps argue AI could actually improve productivity, noting that displaced workers should get the support, training and opportunities they deserve.
The union pointed to two recent local layoffs in the UK, including around 1,000 jobs at Asda’s George division and 450 jobs at Nestlé. These examples, as well as the more than 117,000 tech workers that have been laid off already in 2026 (via layoffs.fyi), put meat on the bones of workers’ fears.
“We can’t just leave it to companies to do the right thing. As we all know, their priority will almost always be their bottom line,” one worker told GMB.
Sign up to the TechRadar Pro newsletter to get all the top news, opinion, features and guidance your business needs to succeed!
“The Government must legislate to protect workers’ jobs or guarantee retraining or redeployment when change is unavoidable.”
Separate Stack Overflow research also points toward growing AI adoption, noting a 90% year-over-year increase from 31% to 59%, as well as a 164% rise in daily use, indicative of more frequent use and higher trust.
However, even as employers start to integrate autonomous AI agents, the report highlights just how important human workers remain. Three in five rarely or never let agents complete tasks without human oversight (63%).
A similar number (60%) prevent agents from making unapproved system changes, too, with the rise of systems like Claude Code and Codex capable of greater autonomy.
Fears over immediate job losses are also written off, with companies still struggling with the same hurdles they’ve been facing for years, implying AI’s effects on workers could be years away yet. Around four in five are concerned about AI accuracy (82%) and security/privacy risks (77%).
Change is on the horizon
Things could be changing though, with companies beginning to be less worried about the same things they’ve been worried about for years. Though cost still serves as a major barrier for 38%, it’s down from 53% last year.
But even as AI adoption climbs, other studies have suggested we might not be at risk after all.
Separate research from the World Economic Forum expects 170 million new jobs to be created by 2030, even though 92 million could be displaced. With disruption estimated to affect 22% of workers, it could be more about repositioning roles and retraining workers than losing humans altogether.
Looking ahead, the WEF sees AI, data, networks, cybersecurity and technological literacy in general as the most in-demand skills.

Nearly half of UK workers fear job displacement due to increasing artificial intelligence adoption, prompting the GMB Union to call for government intervention and mandatory employee retraining programs.

### Key Points
- GMB Union data shows 48% of UK workers worry about AI-related job losses, while 26% report AI is already performing their daily tasks.
- Approximately 48% of employees express concern that companies are utilizing AI tools for workplace surveillance and performance monitoring.
- Stack Overflow research indicates a 90% year-over-year increase in AI adoption, though 63% of users still require human oversight for agent-led tasks.
- The World Economic Forum projects 92 million jobs could be displaced by 2030, while simultaneously creating 170 million new roles.
- High-demand future skills include technological literacy, cybersecurity, data management, and AI proficiency.

### Why it Matters
The tension between rising AI integration and worker anxiety highlights a critical need for clear corporate policies and government-backed retraining initiatives. As businesses prioritize efficiency, proactive workforce development is essential to manage the transition and mitigate the risks of widespread job displacement.

results.title: Google just pushed me one step closer to ditching Google Home for Amazon Alexa
results.author: Karandeep Singh
results.content:
Google just pushed me one step closer to ditching Google Home for Amazon Alexa
Google has, in quite literal terms, turned into an AI company. But ironically enough, it has evidently and consistently drifted away from that one product category meant to make AI omnipresent: smart speakers. Google’s indifference towards its smart speaker lineup for the better part of the decade and its declining everyday performance have already led people to question their decision to be in the Google Assistant ecosystem.
While Google recently announced its first smart speaker in several years, it’s still not publicly available. And on top of that, Google has seemingly discontinued the Nest Mini and Nest Audio.
With no replacement for these models, the new Google Home Speaker is still an unknown amount of time away, and given the current poor state of Google Home/Nest speakers, there is little incentive to stick with them. In comparison, Amazon starts to look like a much more serious alternative — one that doesn’t push you into picking up its one and only option.
If you had to choose today, which smart speaker ecosystem would you buy into?
Going from plural to singular
There was a time when Google was serious about smart speakers. The era when we had real choice, even within the Google ecosystem, from speakers of varied sizes depending on how big a room you wanted to fill. Heck, there were even two different smart display sizes to choose from. You could get a display for your kitchen to watch recipe videos or put a simpler, screenless smart speaker in your bedroom to set alarms and timers. There was something for everyone — but gone are those good old days.
You already know that Google hasn’t updated any of its smart speakers or displays in several years. But at least those older models were still available. That too is changing now, with the Nest Mini and Nest Audio running out of stock to make way for the new Home Speaker.
There was something for everyone — but gone are those good old days.
The upcoming Google Home speaker is built from the ground up around Gemini and costs $100, the same as the recently announced Amazon Echo Dot Max. That leaves Google with no budget or more premium options around the new Home Speaker, and that’s a problem.
It’s not that easy a decision
With just a single model to choose from, the Google Home Speaker becomes a no-brainer. It’s the default (and the only) choice that you have in the Google camp. That makes the shopping experience easy, but at the cost of nuance and choice — something that end users actually prefer. I like having the agency to buy the exact device that fits my needs instead of being forced into the only option a company is selling.
I like having the agency to buy the exact device that fits my needs instead of being forced into the only option a company is selling.
The Home Mini and the Nest Mini came out alongside the similarly priced Amazon Echo Dot, all of them frequently available at throwaway prices, to push the idea that you could place one speaker in every room across your house.
Smart speakers back then, and even today, had the sole purpose of being smart and readily available wherever you are, rather than being music-worthy speakers. But if you still wanted high-quality audio and the smartness on the same device, Google did have pricier options.
That was the very purpose of having a wide range of smart speakers that cater to everyone’s needs, from basic $20 speakers that offer the same AI capabilities to a boombox-sized Google Home Max that could literally power an entire party. I’ve been patiently waiting for Google to launch a new smart display, but we’re still stuck with the aging 2021 model.
Today, none of that has survived but one.
Amazon is serious about the smart home
I’ve stuck with Google smart speakers for nearly ten years now, but that loyalty can only go so far. It’s almost as if it’s in Google’s DNA to find a new media trend and jump on it while forgetting everything else it was working on. While I had imagined AI would propel Google to work on its smart speaker lineup with renewed dedication, it has instead pushed it in the opposite direction. Amazon, on the other hand, has continued to churn out newer models with full commitment.
It was only a few months ago that Amazon introduced its Echo Dot Max and Echo Studio, which are designed around Alexa+, its agentic spruce-up of the vanilla Alexa experience. You can still get the good-old $50 Echo Dot if you want just the basic AI smarts in your room. Amazon also recently updated its Echo Show lineup with 8- and 11-inch models. I’m not particularly fond of them, especially since they lack YouTube integration, but that’s more of a platform issue that hasn’t stopped Amazon from launching new products year on year.
Amazon has a fuller array of smart speakers where you'll find something for yourself, quite unlike Google's.
My favorite Echo speaker, however, is the Echo Spot, which has a tiny color display that shows just the time and other basic details, making it a perfect privacy-forward nightstand accessory without a camera. Then there are fun, kid-friendly variants of these speakers, featuring colorful patterns.
The point is that Amazon offers a broader array of smart speakers, so you’ll find something for yourself, unlike what you’ll find from Google.

- You can also set us as a preferred source in Google Search by clicking the button below.
[preferred_source_box] [/preferred_source_box]
Amazon has got what I need — mostly
Google Assistant has long been the superior option compared to Alexa. It had a better understanding of the web and didn’t always resort to citing Wikipedia when you asked a question. But that comparison isn’t valid anymore in 2026. In fact, it now works even more against Google’s case, since Gemini for smart speakers has so far had a very limited rollout. (It doesn’t seem like Google is very keen on bringing it to me here in India.)
That was the only incentive I had to stay with Google Home speakers, but not anymore. The drying-up stock of the Nest Mini and Nest Audio without a proper replacement is indicative of Google’s neglect for smart speakers as a category. The situation is particularly frustrating for me because neither the new Home Speaker is coming here nor is the new Gemini experience for current models in sight.
The drying-up stock of the Nest Mini and Nest Audio without a proper replacement is indicative of Google's neglect for smart speakers as a category.
Ever since generative AI took over the entirety of Google, smart speakers have been left unattended to the point that their existing experience is only going downhill. I now frequently need to repeat or rephrase my commands for my Google Home Mini to act correctly, which wasn’t the case until a few years ago. Alexa, even without its Plus upgrade, doesn’t face these fundamental issues. On top of that, it is much better at understanding commands in Indian languages, something my mom, who is more comfortable with Hindi, finds useful.
My eagerness to jump to the Alexa camp has little to do with Alexa being the objectively better assistant and more to do with the pitiful state that Google Home speakers are in. The ever-shrinking smart speaker lineup is further evidence that Google is entirely to blame for my decision.
results.summary:
Google is phasing out its Nest Mini and Nest Audio smart speakers, leaving users with limited hardware options and prompting many to consider switching to Amazon’s diverse Alexa ecosystem.

### Key Points
- Google has discontinued the Nest Mini and Nest Audio, leaving no budget or premium alternatives currently available for consumers.
- The upcoming Google Home Speaker, priced at $100, is the company's only planned hardware release, signaling a shift toward a singular product strategy.
- Amazon continues to expand its smart home lineup with varied devices like the Echo Dot, Echo Studio, and updated Echo Show displays.
- Users report declining performance and reliability issues with existing Google Home devices, contrasting with Amazon’s consistent hardware updates and language support.
- Google’s integration of Gemini into smart speakers has seen a limited rollout, further reducing the incentive for long-term users to remain in the ecosystem.

### Why it Matters
The consolidation of Google’s smart home hardware into a single, limited offering risks alienating users who prefer diverse device options for different household needs. This strategic shift highlights a growing market divergence where Amazon’s commitment to hardware variety increasingly challenges Google’s dominance in the smart assistant space.

results.title: Privacy as a Fundamental Right? The Government’s Terrible Privacy Track Record Suggests Virtue Signalling Over a Genuine Commitment
results.author: Michael Geist
results.content:
The government is set to introduce its long-promised privacy reform legislation early this week, with the recognition of a fundamental right to privacy expected to serve as a foundational element of the bill. Establishing privacy as a fundamental right would be a welcome and long-overdue development, one that many have called for and that was set to be added to Bill C-27, the prior attempt at privacy reform. Yet the framing is difficult to square with the government’s actual record on privacy, which over the past year has involved a steady stream of privacy-invasive measures that leave the fundamental rights rhetoric feeling more like virtue signalling than a genuine commitment. Simply put, the government cannot credibly claim to treat privacy as a fundamental right while actively undermining that right through a series of other bills and efforts to sideline the Privacy Commissioner of Canada.
Just over a year ago, the Carney government got off to a poor start on privacy with a pair of bills that left me asking, “What is with this government and privacy?” Bill C-2, the border measures bill introduced in June 2025, buried lawful access provisions unrelated to the border, including a warrantless information-demand power astonishing in its breadth that stood in direct contradiction to the Supreme Court of Canada’s privacy jurisprudence in decisions such as Spencer. The provisions attracted such a backlash that the government eventually removed them and reintroduced the border measures on its own. Days later, it introduced Bill C-4, an “affordability measures” bill that buried an exemption shielding federal political parties from provincial privacy law and made it retroactive to the year 2000. In other words, the instinct from the outset was to bury privacy-invasive measures in unrelated legislation and hope that few Canadians would notice.
If those early bills signalled a government with little regard for privacy, the situation today is worse, with more privacy threats moving through Parliament at once than under any government in recent memory. Bill C-34, the Safe Social Media Act introduced last week, pairs an under-16 social media ban with an age verification mandate that will require tens of millions of Canadians to place their privacy at risk by verifying their age to use services such as Facebook, Instagram and Reddit. Bill C-22, the lawful access bill now under clause-by-clause review, would impose mandatory metadata retention requiring providers to store data about the communications of all their users regardless of whether they are suspected of anything, compel the development of interception capabilities that place encryption at risk, and lower the evidentiary standard for access to subscriber information. And Bill C-25, the government’s second attempt at political party privacy rules after C-4, would still leave the parties subject to weaker privacy obligations than practically any other organization in the country.
The treatment of the Privacy Commissioner is particularly revealing, since a fundamental right to privacy means little without an independent regulator with the power and the standing to defend it. During the clause-by-clause review of Bill C-22, the committee invited the RCMP and CSIS, the agencies that lobbied for the new powers to guide MPs through the amendments, yet Liberal MPs have blocked efforts to ask Commissioner Philippe Dufresne to return to explain his recommended changes. The same disregard is evident in Bill C-34, where the government included a mandatory requirement to consult the Privacy Commissioner on the guidelines and regulations that will define age verification, only for officials to describe a plan that will bring the ban into force without the mandatory consultations.
The government’s underlying attitude toward privacy may be the most telling of all. Bill C-25 moved to shut down meaningful study, restricting the committee’s examination to three days in what appears to be a calculated bet that few Canadians would pay attention to a set of embarrassingly weak privacy rules. And on Bill C-22, when Conservative MP Jacob Mantle pressed the privacy implications of the bill during clause-by-clause, Liberal MP Sima Acan this week responded that the bill “has nothing to do with the privacy of people and their information,” a statement that, after months of hearings on mandatory metadata retention, weakened encryption, and warrantless access to personal information, is difficult to describe as anything other than ill-informed and suggests a government that does not conceive of privacy the way most Canadians do.
The new privacy bill deserves to be evaluated on its merits and if the forthcoming bill delivers a genuine fundamental right to privacy backed by meaningful enforcement, that will be welcome. But legislation does not arise in a vacuum and adding language proclaiming a “fundamental right to privacy” while actively undermining that privacy can’t be ignored. The government may talk the talk on privacy, but it is time for it to walk the walk as well.
results.summary:
The Canadian government is preparing to introduce new privacy reform legislation this week, aiming to establish privacy as a fundamental right despite ongoing criticism regarding its current legislative record.

### Key Points
- The upcoming bill seeks to codify privacy as a fundamental right, a move previously proposed in the stalled Bill C-27.
- Critics argue the government’s actions, including Bill C-22 and Bill C-34, contradict this rhetoric by mandating metadata retention and age verification requirements.
- Bill C-22 proposes lowering evidentiary standards for accessing subscriber information and requires providers to store user communication data.
- Bill C-34 introduces an age verification mandate for social media platforms like Facebook, Instagram, and Reddit, raising significant data security concerns.
- The government has faced accusations of sidelining Privacy Commissioner Philippe Dufresne by blocking his testimony on key legislative amendments.
- Bill C-25 continues to face scrutiny for providing federal political parties with weaker privacy obligations than those required of other organizations.

### Why it Matters
The proposed legislation faces skepticism because it coincides with multiple government initiatives that critics claim actively erode digital privacy and weaken regulatory oversight. Whether this bill provides substantive protections or serves as symbolic policy will determine the future of data rights and government accountability in Canada.
